gist: A history of Taiwan's two-decade, multi-billion-dollar failure to break into DRAM — Vanguard, TI-Acer, Nanya, Powerchip, ProMOS, Winbond, Rexchip and the failed Taiwan Memory Company — explaining why a foundry-strong island never became a memory power, and why DRAM's commodity bullwhip cycles, licensed (not owned) IP, and short-term bank-debt financing repeatedly washed out sub-scale players while Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron consolidated the industry to a profitable oligopoly.

themes.theme: Why DRAM is structurally different from logic/foundry

insights.claim: DRAM is a volatile commodity with economics nothing like logic chips: in its early decades the cycle ran roughly four good years to one bad year, and success required flawless R&D scaled every two-to-three nodes or you fall out of the race entirely.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Memory is a capital-intensive commodity oligopoly; investors should treat DRAM names (Micron, Samsung, SK Hynix, Nanya) as deep cyclicals tied to the node-cadence race, not as steady compounders like a foundry.
insights.quote: DRAM is a famously ferocious industry, with economic dynamics nothing like logic.

insights.claim: In DRAM the majority of a chip's nominal accounting cost is depreciation (sunk cost) while actual cash cost is low, so once cash costs are covered producers have little incentive to cut output even as added supply crashes prices further.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Explains why DRAM downturns overshoot to the downside — the cost structure removes the natural supply brake, so investors should expect price crashes well below 'rational' levels before capacity discipline returns.
insights.quote: If those are covered, memory-makers have little incentive to cut back on production even if producing will cause prices to crash even more.

insights.claim: Unlike a pure-play foundry (TSMC) whose customers bring their own designs, a DRAM maker must own and design its own memory IP — making licensed-IP DRAM businesses structurally weaker than they appear.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: IP ownership, not just fab capacity, is the moat in memory; a 'manufacturing-only' DRAM strategy is a red flag for durability and margins.
insights.quote: But memory-makers must design their memory, and it is a critical part of their success.

themes.theme: The bullwhip / chicken-game cycle as the core investor lesson

insights.claim: DRAM supercycles are repeatedly triggered by Windows OS upgrades that raised RAM requirements — Windows 3.0 (1990), Windows 95 (4-8MB), and Windows Vista (2006) each fooled makers into over-building fabs in the belief 'this time is different'.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Demand-side catalysts (today: AI/datacenter, HBM) recur and reliably induce over-investment; the playbook is to fade peak-cycle capex euphoria, because the supply response lags and overshoots.
insights.quote: Such times often snooker memory-makers into thinking that things are different this time so they go build a bunch of expensive factories.

insights.claim: The mid-1990s DRAM build-out saw worldwide fab count leap from 73 in 1995 to 170 in 1996, with nine new DRAM fabs in Taiwan alone, and five more entering in 1997 — a textbook bullwhip that cratered the market.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Capacity decisions made years earlier keep arriving after prices have already turned; watch fab-count and capex commitments as a leading bear signal for memory pricing.
insights.quote: Worldwide, the number of fabs leapt from 73 in 1995 to 170 in 1996.

insights.claim: From 1995's $40 billion peak the world DRAM market collapsed to under $15 billion by 1998 — a >60% drawdown — as 4-megabit prices fell from $12 to $7.50 to $3.40 to $2.30, below Vanguard's cash cost.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Quantifies the amplitude of memory cycles — peak-to-trough market value can fall 60%+ in three years, sizing the downside risk for any DRAM-exposed position.
insights.quote: By 1998, the value of the whole market had cratered to under $15 billion, down over 60% from the sweet summer days of 1995.

insights.claim: Semiconductor demand signals take time to travel up and down the supply chain (the 'Beer Game' / bullwhip effect), so end-user demand changes are amplified into violent over- and under-supply at producers like Samsung and TSMC.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for timing chip cycles: the further upstream you sit, the larger the demand swings you experience — relevant to the current AI-driven memory shortage.
insights.quote: the time it takes for these signals to travel up or down the supply chain can cause a painful “bullwhip” effect.

themes.theme: Why Taiwanese DRAM players structurally lost

insights.claim: Taiwanese DRAM firms almost all licensed foreign IP (Oki, Mitsubishi, Toshiba, Siemens, Infineon, Elpida, Micron) rather than owning it — paying royalties that thinned margins and leaving them stranded when a partner failed to advance, as when Oki couldn't move Nanya to the next generation.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Royalty-dependence is a recurring failure mode in commodity semis; favor memory players that own leading-edge process IP over licensees.
insights.quote: Licensing someone else's DRAM IP means paying them a percentage, cutting into precious margins.

insights.claim: Taiwanese DRAM makers funded their fab builds with short-term bank debt, unlike Samsung which could lean on end-product profits and chaebol cash inflows — making Taiwan the most fragile cohort in every downturn.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Balance-sheet structure determines who survives a memory crash; integrated/diversified producers with internal cash outlast debt-funded pure-plays — a durable screen for cyclical survivors.
insights.quote: the Taiwanese funded their builds with short-term bank debt. A disastrous move.

insights.claim: Despite holding only 13-15% of world DRAM share, Taiwanese firms absorbed 42% of the industry's ~$10 billion in losses between Q1 2007 and Q3 2008 — grossly outsized losses relative to scale.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Sub-scale, high-cost producers bear a disproportionate share of cycle losses; market share alone understates a weak player's downside concentration.
insights.quote: Taiwanese DRAM firms took 42% of those losses despite having between 13-15% of world market share.

insights.claim: Morris Chang opposed both ITRI's and TI-Acer's DRAM ventures on principle because neither owned core memory technology — ITRI started from scratch and TI-Acer merely licensed TI's tech — and he privately judged the 'tragic outcome was almost inevitable'.
insights.speaker: Morris Chang (via Jon Y)
insights.so_what: The most successful operator in the industry flagged the no-owned-IP model as doomed from the start — a high-credibility warning sign worth generalizing to today's aspirants (e.g. CXMT).
insights.quote: the "tragic outcome was almost inevitable".

insights.claim: Government designation of DRAM as a 'nationally important' field under the Two Trillion policy created an implicit bailout guarantee and moral hazard, encouraging firms to over-build on bank debt expecting rescue if they failed.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
entities: Two Trillion and Twin Star policy
insights.so_what: Industrial-policy 'strategic' labels can distort capital allocation and worsen over-investment; an implicit-guarantee dynamic is a tell for eventual taxpayer-funded cleanups and shareholder wipeouts.
insights.quote: created an implicit government guarantee. Companies moved ahead with their plans thinking that the government would bail them out if they failed. A moral hazard.

themes.theme: TSMC, Vanguard and the foundry pivot as the winning move

insights.claim: TSMC's first CEO Jim Dykes refused PC-maker pressure to make DRAMs during the 1988 shortage and kept TSMC purely in logic — a discipline that preserved the pure-play foundry model that became the industry's most valuable franchise.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Strategic focus (logic only) beat diversification into commodity memory; the discipline to say no to a hot adjacent market is itself a moat indicator.
insights.quote: Dykes refused, fully believing in the approach of staying only in logic chips.

insights.claim: TSMC absorbed the failed TI-Acer DRAM venture in 1999, converting its fab into Fab 7, and combined with the Worldwide Semiconductor acquisition this boosted TSMC's wafer capacity ahead of rival UMC — a major turning point in the foundry race.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Failed memory assets became cheap capacity that helped TSMC pull decisively ahead of UMC — illustrates how one player's distress is another's strategic accretion.
insights.quote: This purchase along that of another foundry challenger called Worldwide Semiconductor helped boost TSMC's wafer capacity ahead of rival UMC.

insights.claim: Vanguard, founded by Morris Chang via auction in 1994 to commercialize ITRI's DRAM project, lost $155 million in 1998 (a then-record corporate loss in Taiwan) before pivoting entirely to logic foundry by 2004 on Rick Tsai's advice that 'memory had no future'.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: The pivot from commodity DRAM to specialty/analog foundry was the survival path for Taiwan's memory exiters — a template repeated by ProMOS and Powerchip/PSMC.
insights.quote: Rick Tsai eventually privately told Chang that memory had no future and suggested that Vanguard pivot to logic foundry.

insights.claim: Vanguard recently licensed TSMC's power-GaN technology as TSMC frees up capacity for AI, and the two remain separate-but-allied with TSMC referring business and transferring process nodes to Vanguard.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Vanguard is positioned as a TSMC-aligned specialty/power-semiconductor play benefiting from TSMC's AI-driven capacity reallocation — a second-order AI beneficiary.
insights.quote: Vanguard even recently licensed TSMC's power GaN technologies as the giant moves to free up more capacity for AI.

insights.claim: ProMOS and Powerchip survived the DRAM collapse by abandoning memory: ProMOS became a fabless designer after selling its fab, and Powerchip spun its fabs into logic foundry PSMC specializing in display driver ICs.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Specialty logic (display drivers, power) was the escape hatch from commodity memory — relevant when assessing how distressed memory assets can be repurposed into viable foundry niches.
insights.quote: Powerchip spun off its fabs to create PSMC, a logic foundry. Their specialty is the display driver IC ... That basically saved their butts.

insights.claim: The DRAM industry consolidated from 20-30 companies historically to just three-to-five today, with 'only three really mattering' — the structural endpoint of decades of cyclical washouts.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: DRAM is now a rational oligopoly (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron); the surviving three should show better pricing discipline and higher through-cycle returns than the boom-bust era implies.
insights.quote: Once upon a time, the DRAM industry involved 20-30 companies. Today that has consolidated to three to five, with only three of them really mattering.

insights.claim: Micron consolidated the wreckage of Taiwan's DRAM industry — buying TI's DRAM business (1998), bankrupt Elpida for $2.5 billion (2012, which carried Elpida's Rexchip JV stake), the rest of Rexchip from Powerchip, and Inotera in 2016 — so the majority of Micron's DRAM is now made in Taiwan with 15,000 employees there.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Micron's Taiwan footprint is the consolidated remains of an entire failed national industry; geopolitical/Taiwan-supply-chain risk to Micron is concretely concentrated, and Micron was the cycle's ultimate winner-by-acquisition.
insights.quote: the majority of its DRAM is made in Taiwan.

insights.claim: Korea's DRAM consolidation came via the Asian Financial Crisis forcing the merger of Hyundai and Goldstar/LG semiconductor divisions into Hynix (today SK Hynix), while Samsung and Hynix refused to cede an inch of share — out-burning the debt-laden Taiwanese.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: The Koreans' willingness and ability to absorb losses to hold share was the decisive competitive weapon — a 'chicken game' that scale and deep pockets win.
insights.quote: with Samsung and Hynix refusing to give up even an inch of market share, there was only so much money the Taiwanese can afford to burn.

insights.claim: Europe's last serious DRAM maker Qimonda (spun out of Infineon to get DRAM volatility off the books) was cut off from sibling support and filed for bankruptcy in 2009 after a German government rescue fell through, with assets sold piecemeal.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Spinning a volatile cyclical out of a diversified parent removed its shock absorber and killed it — a cautionary tale on financial-engineering separations of cyclical units.
insights.quote: this cut off Qimonda from any future support from its siblings - making it vulnerable to a downturn.

themes.theme: The failed Taiwan Memory Company bailout

insights.claim: In March 2009 Taiwan proposed Taiwan Memory Company (TMC) — a government-backed roll-up of Inotera, Nanya, Powerchip, ProMOS, Rexchip and Winbond funded with ~$1 billion to acquire Elpida's tech and rival Samsung/Hynix — but it collapsed because the firms wanted bailouts, not merger, and incompatible Elpida vs Micron technology families made fast consolidation impractical.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: State-led memory consolidation failed on misaligned incentives and incompatible tech stacks — a precedent for skepticism about government-engineered national champions in fragmented cyclical industries.
insights.quote: Taiwan's various DRAM companies did not want to participate. It seems like they more expected handouts of government money (i.e, bailouts) to help them tide over the current situation.

insights.claim: A modest April 2009 DRAM price recovery (driven by Qimonda's collapse plus Elpida/Powerchip pulling supply and buyers restocking) removed the urgency for TMC, and Taiwan's Legislative Yuan ultimately voted down even the scaled-back $150 million Taiwan Innovation Memory Company as a fruitless giveaway to a risky cyclical industry.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Cyclical recoveries routinely kill the political will for structural reform mid-crisis, locking in fragmentation — a pattern that recurs across distressed-industry bailout debates.
insights.quote: The market recovery - precarious as it was - nevertheless took away much of the TMC project's urgency.

themes.theme: Nanya as the lone survivor and AI-cycle read-through

insights.claim: Nanya Technology is the only major Taiwanese player to survive while staying in DRAM, kept alive by Formosa Plastics conglomerate money, and now operates as a second-tier supplier behind the big three and China's CXMT.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Nanya's survival hinged entirely on a deep-pocketed conglomerate backstop — and CXMT now occupies the rising-challenger slot Taiwan once held, a new entrant to watch as a future supply disruptor.
insights.quote: The only major Taiwanese player to survive while remaining in DRAM was Nanya Technology, which only made it through thanks to money from the Formosa Plastics conglomerate.

insights.claim: Nanya recently broke ground on a new 10-nanometer-class fab in New Taipei City (Fab 5A) equipped with an EUV machine, timed into the current AI-fueled DRAM shortage, with 2026 revenues up roughly 600% year-over-year.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: A 600% YoY revenue surge and a first EUV-equipped advanced fab make Nanya a concrete, high-torque way to play the current AI-driven memory upcycle — though as a perennial second-tier laggard the cyclical risk cuts both ways.
insights.quote: Their 2026 revenues year to date are up something like 600% year-over-year.

insights.claim: Winbond and Macronix retreated from commodity DRAM back to NOR flash (where they remain dominant) plus logic and custom memory, with Winbond still making a little specialty DRAM — the niche-memory survival path.
insights.speaker: Jon Y
insights.so_what: Specialty/niche memory (NOR flash, custom, specialty DRAM) is a defensible escape from commodity DRAM; Winbond and Macronix are NOR-flash-dominant names rather than mainstream DRAM exposure.
insights.quote: The two of them remain a dominant player in NOR flash.
